---
title: "About Canada Observatory"
subtitle: "Mission & method"
---

## Purpose

Canada Observatory — CanObs for short — brings the key facts about Canada together in one place — and keeps them there.

Reliable data about the country already exists, but it tends to be scattered: spread across dozens of government agencies and international databases, or published as one-off charts in articles that can be hard to find again weeks later. CanObs gathers it into a single, consistent, continually updated reference with interactive charts you can explore — a place you *know* you can come back to: to check the latest figure, link to a chart, or settle a question when it comes up with friends, family, or colleagues.

It is a neutral, evidence-focused project, free and open to everyone. It aims to inform civic conversation with clear, quantified data — without policy advocacy or partisan framing.

## What makes Canada Observatory different

- **Every number in context.** Each indicator is shown alongside a group of 17 comparable OECD countries — not in isolation — so you can see not just what a figure is, but how Canada compares.
- **Broad coverage in one place.** Six themes — people & society, the economy and affordability, government and public finances, health and well-being, the land and environment, and education, science, and innovation — all under one roof and presented the same way.
- **Descriptive, not prescriptive.** CanObs presents the data and the comparison without partisan or policy framing. Where a widely recognized benchmark exists — such as the Bank of Canada's inflation target — it is shown for context; otherwise, what the numbers mean is left to you.
- **Built to stay current — and easy to share.** The site refreshes automatically every week from authoritative sources — with some series updated on a longer cycle, as each source releases — and every chart shows the date of its most recent data. Every chart has its own stable link you can copy, bookmark, and share — with no paywall and no login.
- **Down to the neighbourhood.** Where the data allows, national figures are complemented by maps that reach all the way to the census-tract and dissemination-area level, so you can find your own community.
- **Open and verifiable.** The data comes only from authoritative primary sources, every chart cites where its numbers come from, the cleaned datasets are free to download, and the code is open source.
- **Independent.** CanObs has no commercial sponsor, political affiliation, or advocacy agenda. The data, the peer comparisons, and the official benchmarks do the work.

## One deliberate exception

CanObs takes no policy positions — with a single exception: we advocate for
the **creation, use, and open availability of public data** itself. A project
like this can only exist because public agencies collect rigorous data and
publish it openly, and we say so plainly. Read why on
[Open Data: The One Cause](about/open-data.qmd).

## The peer group

For international comparisons, CanObs uses a set of 17 comparable OECD economies:

**G7:** Canada, United States, United Kingdom, Germany, France, Italy, Japan

**Extended peers:** Australia, South Korea, Netherlands, Sweden, Switzerland, Norway, Denmark, Finland, Israel, New Zealand

This broader set provides more meaningful context than the G7 alone, which excludes important peers.

## How the site works

- Data is fetched and cleaned by automated Python scripts that run weekly.
- Source agencies refresh at different cadences — population estimates
  quarterly, most OECD series annually, the CPI monthly — and some report with
  a lag of a year or more. Each chart notes its source and the date of its most
  recent observation.
- If a source is temporarily unavailable when the pipeline runs, the previous
  data is kept in place rather than overwritten, so a chart never goes blank
  because of a transient outage.
- Charts use consistent styling: Canada is highlighted in red, peer countries
  in grey, with a small set of named comparators in colour.

## Where the data comes from

Every series on this site comes from an authoritative primary source —
Statistics Canada, the OECD, Environment and Climate Change Canada, the World
Bank, the Bank of Canada, and others — under open licences (with one
charts-only exception, noted there). The full list, with what each source is
used for and its licence, is on the [Data Sources](about/data-sources.qmd) page.

## Open data downloads

All cleaned data files are freely available for download in CSV format from the
[GitHub repository](https://github.com/CanadaObservatory/CanadaDataObs). They
are intended to be usable by students, journalists, researchers, and anyone
else who wants to work with Canadian data in a simple, accessible format.

## People & code

Canada Observatory is an independent project — see [People](about/people.qmd) for who is
behind it, and [Sites We Like](about/related.qmd) for the projects we admire
and consult. The source code is open and available on
[GitHub](https://github.com/CanadaObservatory/CanadaDataObs).
